guest_credentials: Coach and scientist at Science to Sport, specializes in training periodization, testing protocols, and athlete development
key_claims: There are only three training levers: intensity, frequency, and duration — all coaching comes down to these regardless of coach quality
Zone 1 training provides significant aerobic benefit with almost zero residual fatigue, enabling better recovery and harder sessions the next day
The 80/20 polarization principle (80% easy, 20% hard) is used across different periodization approaches with minimal outcome differences between traditional, reverse, and random intensity allocation
Intramuscular triglyceride rides in carb-fasted state (with protein) can improve metabolic flexibility; zone 1 rides can be done with 30-40g carbs or fully fasted depending on athlete tolerance
Wearable data (HRV, heart rate) is incomplete without context; subjective feeling and Rate of Perceived Exertion (RPE) scale are more accurate indicators than metrics alone
specific_numbers: 30-40 grams of carbs for zone 1-2 rides
80-120 grams of carbs per hour for endurance sessions
Fat max intervals: 300-340 watts, sometimes 280-330 watts
Resting heart rate increased from 34 to 43 (9 bpm increase with fatigue)
90 minutes of glycogen storage in muscles and liver
Four-week blocks for amateur athletes (case dependent)
cluster_reasoning: Episode is entirely focused on training methodology (intensity, frequency, duration, periodization, zone allocation) with Daryl Fitzgerald discussing the fundamental levers of coaching; nutrition and recovery appear as supporting topics within the training framework rather than primary pillars.
